name: Damage Numbers
description: Floating or anchored numbers that communicate damage, healing, shields, critical hits, or resisted effects.
design_notes: Damage numbers can teach combat math, sell impact, and reward optimization, but they can also clutter the screen. Decide which combat results deserve numbers, how long they stay visible, and whether players can reduce or disable them.
design_purpose: Confirm combat results and relative attack strength.
Differentiate damage types, healing, shields, critical hits, and resisted values.
Support build tuning and player learning without requiring logs.
player_fantasy: The player feels their attacks have measurable impact and can read combat outcomes quickly.

parameters.name: number_lifetime_ms
parameters.description: How long a number remains visible before fading.

parameters.name: stack_merge_window_ms
parameters.description: Window for combining rapid repeated damage into one number.

parameters.name: critical_scale_multiplier
parameters.description: Size multiplier for critical or weak-point values.

parameters.name: max_simultaneous_numbers
parameters.description: Upper bound before values are merged, culled, or summarized.

parameters.name: world_space_drift_pixels
parameters.description: Screen drift applied over the number lifetime.

edge_cases: Damage over time and area attacks can create dozens of numbers per second unless merged or rate-limited.
Large enemies need anchor points so numbers do not appear inside geometry or offscreen.
Negative, immune, absorbed, overheal, and blocked results need different display rules.
Localization and font choices must handle separators, abbreviations, and right-to-left text where supported.
common_bugs: Numbers spawn from raw collision events and appear even when damage was blocked.
World-space labels sort behind walls or enemy meshes.
Stacked values merge across different targets because the grouping key is incomplete.
Critical number animation hides the exact value before the player can read it.
balancing_notes: Verbose numbers encourage optimization and build comparison; minimal numbers preserve immersion and reduce clutter.
Showing resisted or absorbed values helps players understand enemy defenses without external spreadsheets.
accessibility_notes: Provide size, opacity, motion, and disable options for floating numbers.
Use text labels or icons for damage categories instead of color alone.

implementation_notes.unity: Bind the UI to gameplay state through events or observable view models, not direct polling from presentation widgets; support UI Toolkit, uGUI, and input glyph updates from the active Input System scheme.
implementation_notes.godot: Drive Control nodes from a small view-model dictionary or signal payload, and keep input focus, localization, and scaling separate from gameplay state.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use UMG or CommonUI widgets fed by gameplay events, with focus navigation, platform glyphs, and replicated-state boundaries handled outside widget animation code.
implementation_notes.web: Render the UI from normalized game state, keep input mapping and localization data outside component logic, and avoid making animation completion the source of truth.

example_games.title: Diablo III
example_games.note: Uses floating damage numbers to show combat output and critical hits.

example_games.title: Borderlands 2
example_games.note: Uses damage numbers to communicate weapon and elemental effects.
